turns.content: I'm trying to optimize my Rust structs for cache locality and SIMD. I've read about using #[repr(align(N))] with N being a power of two, but I'm not sure if this is always beneficial. Can you explain when and how it works?

Sure! When working with Rust structs, alignment controls where data sits in memory relative to the hardware cache lines. Using #[repr(align(N))] with N as a power of two can help avoid false sharing — multiple variables in one cache line that get updated frequently can cause contention and slow down performance. However, over-aligning might waste space if you don't need such high alignment.
Okay, so I should align structs to cache lines (64 bytes) for better performance?

Yes, that's a good practice. But be cautious: if N is too large, you might increase struct sizes unnecessarily. It’s often best to profile and see where contention actually occurs before aligning aggressively. For SIMD, alignment of 16 or 32 bytes can help vector instructions work efficiently.
I'm trying to understand why in Rust, sometimes structs use #[repr(align(N))] with N larger than the natural alignment. Can you explain when and why this is useful?
Sure! In Rust, `#[repr(align(N))]` lets you specify that a struct's memory layout should be aligned on an `N`-byte boundary. This can be useful for optimizing cache performance or SIMD operations.
Oh, I see. So if the natural alignment is 8 bytes (because of longs), but we set align(16), does that mean every instance will take up more space?

Yes! When you increase alignment with `#[repr(align(N))]`, each struct instance will be padded so it starts on an N-byte boundary. This means the total size of the struct is rounded up to a multiple of N. For example, if your natural alignment is 8 bytes but you set align(16), instances might take up more space.
So why would I want that? Can I just use `align(N)` for everything?

Actually, no. Alignment must be a power of two and it affects the size of your struct. If you set align(16), but your data types only require 8-byte alignment, your structs will waste space. But if you're doing SIMD operations or want to avoid false sharing (multiple structs on the same cache line interfering), then increasing alignment can be beneficial.
What's the difference between natural alignment and explicit align(N)?

Natural alignment is determined by the largest member of the struct (e.g., a long on x86-64 has 8-byte alignment). Explicit `#[repr(align(N))]` overrides this, always aligning on that boundary. It's useful for fine-grained control over memory layout.
So if I want cache-line aligned structs (say 64 bytes), do I just set `#[repr(align(64))]`?
Yes! But remember, this will increase the size of your struct to be a multiple of 64 bytes. It's good for performance because cache lines are typically 64 bytes.
Wait, what about SIMD? Is that related?

Absolutely! When you're working with SIMD (e.g., using `#[repr(simd)]` or calling AVX2/AVX512 intrinsics), it's important to ensure your data is aligned on the right boundary. Otherwise, the CPU might raise an exception or performance will suffer.
So I should always be careful about alignment when working with SIMD?
Yes! Use `#[repr(align(N))]` where necessary to ensure data is aligned properly. But do keep in mind that excessive alignment can waste memory, so find a balance.
